  • readinessProbe — hits your /health/ready endpoint. If it fails, Kubernetes stops routing traffic to this pod (removes it from the service's load balancer) but doesn't restart it. The pod comes back into rotation when it starts passing again.

This gives you safe rolling deployments: new pods start up, pass readiness, then get traffic. Old pods drain and terminate. No downtime.

questions.tip: Separate liveness from readiness. Liveness should be trivial — just return 200. Readiness should check dependencies (DB, Redis). If you mix them, a DB blip will cause all your pods to restart simultaneously — exactly the wrong time for a restart.

questions.question: How does JSON serialization work in ASP.NET Core? System.Text.Json vs Newtonsoft.Json?
questions.answer:
ASP.NET Core 3.0+ ships with System.Text.Json as the default serialiser, replacing the old dependency on Newtonsoft.Json (Json.NET). I've used both extensively and there are real differences to know.

System.Text.Json (default in .NET 6/7/8)

// Manual serialization
var product = new Product { Id = 1, Name = "Widget", Price = 9.99m };
string json = JsonSerializer.Serialize(product);
// { "Id":1, "Name":"Widget", "Price":9.99 }

// Deserialization
var dto = JsonSerializer.Deserialize<Product>(json);

// Options
var options = new JsonSerializerOptions
{
    PropertyNamingPolicy = JsonNamingPolicy.CamelCase,  // camelCase properties
    WriteIndented = true,                               // pretty print
    DefaultIgnoreCondition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingNull, // skip nulls
    PropertyNameCaseInsensitive = true                 // case-insensitive reads
};

// Attributes
public class Order
{
    [JsonPropertyName("order_id")]     // custom property name in JSON
    public int Id { get; set; }

    [JsonIgnore]                        // never serialize this
    public string InternalNote { get; set; }

    [JsonConverter(typeof(DateTimeOffsetConverter))]
    public DateTimeOffset CreatedAt { get; set; }
}

Configure globally in Program.cs:

builder.Services.AddControllers()
    .AddJsonOptions(opts =>
    {
        opts.JsonSerializerOptions.PropertyNamingPolicy = JsonNamingPolicy.CamelCase;
        opts.JsonSerializerOptions.Converters.Add(new JsonStringEnumConverter());
        opts.JsonSerializerOptions.DefaultIgnoreCondition = JsonIgnoreCondition.WhenWritingNull;
    });

When to switch to Newtonsoft.Json:

// Install: Microsoft.AspNetCore.Mvc.NewtonsoftJson
builder.Services.AddControllers()
    .AddNewtonsoftJson(opts =>
    {
        opts.SerializerSettings.ContractResolver =
            new CamelCasePropertyNamesContractResolver();
        opts.SerializerSettings.NullValueHandling = NullValueHandling.Ignore;
        opts.SerializerSettings.ReferenceLoopHandling = ReferenceLoopHandling.Ignore;
    });

System.Text.Json vs Newtonsoft — real differences:

  • Performance: System.Text.Json is faster and uses less memory — it was built for performance from the ground up.
  • Features: Newtonsoft has more features — dynamic serialisation, LINQ to JSON (JObject/JArray), better handling of edge cases, polymorphic serialisation without extra config.
  • Circular references: Newtonsoft handles these natively with ReferenceLoopHandling.Ignore. System.Text.Json needs ReferenceHandler.Preserve or you restructure the DTO.
  • Migration friction: Existing projects using Newtonsoft-specific features need effort to migrate. I don't migrate unless there's a performance need.

My rule: New projects use System.Text.Json. Legacy projects stay on Newtonsoft. If I need JObject/dynamic JSON manipulation, Newtonsoft wins.

followUps.question: How do you handle polymorphic serialization (serialize derived types) in System.Text.Json?
followUps.answer:
// .NET 7+ -- built-in polymorphic serialization
[JsonPolymorphic]
[JsonDerivedType(typeof(Circle), typeDiscriminator: "circle")]
[JsonDerivedType(typeof(Rectangle), typeDiscriminator: "rectangle")]
public abstract class Shape { public string Color { get; set; } }
public class Circle : Shape { public double Radius { get; set; } }
public class Rectangle : Shape { public double Width { get; set; } public double Height { get; set; } }

// Serializes with $type discriminator:
// { "$type": "circle", "Radius": 5.0, "Color": "Red" }

Before .NET 7, this required a custom JsonConverter or switching to Newtonsoft. In .NET 7+, it's first-class support.

questions.tip: Performance tip: If you're serializing a lot in hot paths, use System.Text.Json's source generation ([JsonSerializable] attribute) to generate serializer code at compile time, eliminating runtime reflection entirely.

questions.question: How do you improve ASP.NET Core Web API performance? What's your checklist?
questions.answer:
Performance tuning is something I approach methodically — measure first, optimise second. Here's the checklist I go through:

1. Database — usually the biggest win

  • Add indexes on columns used in WHERE, JOIN, ORDER BY clauses. Check execution plans for table scans.
  • Use AsNoTracking() in EF Core for read-only queries — no change tracking overhead.
  • Fix N+1 problems with Include() / ThenInclude().
  • Select only what you need: .Select(x => new { x.Id, x.Name }) instead of loading the whole entity.
  • Use Dapper for complex reporting queries where EF Core's generated SQL is inefficient.

2. Caching

  • Cache expensive DB queries with IMemoryCache or Redis. Even a 30-second cache on a product list can eliminate thousands of queries.
  • Use Response Caching for GET endpoints that don't change per-user.
  • Cache computed results (reports, aggregations) in the background via IHostedService.

3. Async all the way

  • All I/O (DB, HTTP calls, file reads) should be async — never block with .Result or .Wait().
  • Use Task.WhenAll to run independent async operations in parallel instead of sequentially.

4. HTTP and API design

  • Enable GZIP/Brotli response compression — can reduce response size by 70-80%.
  • Use IAsyncEnumerable + streaming for large result sets instead of loading everything into memory.
  • Pagination on all list endpoints — never return unbounded lists.
// Response compression
builder.Services.AddResponseCompression(opts =>
{
    opts.EnableForHttps = true;
    opts.Providers.Add<BrotliCompressionProvider>();
    opts.Providers.Add<GzipCompressionProvider>();
});
app.UseResponseCompression();

// Streaming large datasets
[HttpGet("export")]
public async IAsyncEnumerable<OrderDto> ExportOrders(
    [EnumeratorCancellation] CancellationToken ct)
{
    await foreach (var order in _db.Orders.AsAsyncEnumerable().WithCancellation(ct))
        yield return new OrderDto(order); // streams row by row, low memory
}

5. Reduce allocations in hot paths

  • Use Span<T> and Memory<T> to avoid array copies in parsing/processing code.
  • Use StringBuilder for string concatenation in loops.
  • Use object pools (ObjectPool<T>) for frequently created objects.
  • Run a profiler (dotMemory, PerfView, BenchmarkDotNet) before optimising — don't guess.

6. Connection pooling

SQL Server and HttpClient connections are pooled by default. Don't create new SqlConnection or HttpClient per request — use DI-injected DbContext (scoped) and IHttpClientFactory.

7. Output caching (ASP.NET Core 7+)

builder.Services.AddOutputCache();
app.UseOutputCache();

[HttpGet]
[OutputCache(Duration = 60, VaryByQueryKeys = new[] { "category" })]
public async Task<IActionResult> GetProducts(string category) { ... }

followUps.question: How do you measure performance issues in ASP.NET Core?
followUps.answer:
I never guess at performance problems — I measure:

  • Application Insights / Datadog / Jaeger — distributed tracing in production. Shows which endpoint is slow and why (DB calls, external calls, CPU).
  • MiniProfiler — dev-time profiling, shows DB query count and timing inline on every page/API response. Great for catching N+1 on the spot.
  • BenchmarkDotNet — micro-benchmarks to compare two implementations (e.g., System.Text.Json vs Newtonsoft speed).
  • dotnet-counters / dotnet-trace — CLI tools for live production metrics (GC, thread pool, request queue).
  • EF Core logging — set log level to Debug to see all SQL generated. Catching a query that generates 200 SQL statements is usually the first performance fix.

questions.tip: Order of impact: Database (indexes, N+1) > Caching > Async > HTTP compression > Memory allocations. Fix the database first. Nine times out of ten, a slow API is a slow query.

questions.question: How do you store and read configuration in ASP.NET Core? Where should DB connection strings live?
questions.answer:
This comes up in almost every interview and in every project — where to put config and how to read it safely. I've learned the hard way that putting connection strings in source code or even in appsettings.json committed to a repo is a mistake you only make once.

Configuration sources — read in priority order (each overrides the previous):

  1. appsettings.json — base config, safe for non-sensitive defaults.
  2. appsettings.{Environment}.json — environment-specific overrides (Development, Staging, Production).
  3. Environment variables — great for containers and CI/CD.
  4. User Secrets (dev only) — local machine secrets, never committed.
  5. Azure Key Vault / AWS Secrets Manager — production secrets.
  6. Command-line arguments — highest priority, useful for deployment overrides.

appsettings.json (non-sensitive config):

{
  "ConnectionStrings": {
    "Default": "Server=(localdb)\\mssqllocaldb;Database=MyApp;Trusted_Connection=true"
  },
  "Jwt": {
    "Issuer": "https://myapp.com",
    "Audience": "https://myapp.com"
  },
  "FeatureFlags": {
    "EnableNewCheckout": true
  },
  "Logging": {
    "LogLevel": {
      "Default": "Information",
      "Microsoft.AspNetCore": "Warning"
    }
  }
}

User Secrets — dev machine only, never in git:

# One-time setup per project
dotnet user-secrets init

# Store a secret
dotnet user-secrets set "ConnectionStrings:Default" "Server=dev-server;Password=real-password"
dotnet user-secrets set "Jwt:Secret" "my-super-secret-key"

# Stored in %APPDATA%/Microsoft/UserSecrets/{project-id}/secrets.json
# Never in the project folder, never in git

Reading configuration in code:

// Option 1: IConfiguration (direct key access)
public class SomeService
{
    public SomeService(IConfiguration config)
    {
        var connStr = config.GetConnectionString("Default");
        var issuer  = config["Jwt:Issuer"];               // nested with :
        var enabled = config.GetValue<bool>("FeatureFlags:EnableNewCheckout");
    }
}

// Option 2: IOptions<T> (strongly typed, preferred for complex settings)
public class JwtSettings
{
    public string Issuer   { get; set; }
    public string Audience { get; set; }
    public string Secret   { get; set; }
}

// Program.cs -- bind config section to a class
builder.Services.Configure<JwtSettings>(builder.Configuration.GetSection("Jwt"));

// Inject and use
public class TokenService
{
    private readonly JwtSettings _settings;
    public TokenService(IOptions<JwtSettings> opts) => _settings = opts.Value;
}

Production — Azure Key Vault:

// Install: Azure.Extensions.AspNetCore.Configuration.Secrets
// Uses Managed Identity -- no password stored anywhere
if (builder.Environment.IsProduction())
{
    builder.Configuration.AddAzureKeyVault(
        new Uri($"https://my-vault.vault.azure.net/"),
        new DefaultAzureCredential()); // uses Managed Identity
}
// Secrets in Key Vault override appsettings.json values

followUps.question: What is the difference between IConfiguration, IOptions<T>, IOptionsSnapshot<T>, and IOptionsMonitor<T>?
followUps.answer:
• IConfiguration — raw config access by key string. No type safety. Good for one-off reads.
  • IOptions<T> — singleton lifetime, reads config once at startup. Use for settings that don't change while the app runs.
  • IOptionsSnapshot<T> — scoped lifetime, re-reads config per request. Use when config might change (e.g., appsettings.json updated) and you want the new value on the next request. Only available in scoped/transient services.
  • IOptionsMonitor<T> — singleton, notifies you when config changes via OnChange() callback. Use in background services or any singleton that needs to react to live config changes.

questions.tip: Never commit secrets to git. Rotate them immediately if you do. Use User Secrets for dev, environment variables for CI/CD and containers, Azure Key Vault for production. The 'Jwt:Secret' key should never appear in any committed file.

questions.question: Why do we use Kestrel if IIS already exists? What is a reverse proxy?
questions.answer:
This is a question that trips up a lot of people — they assume Kestrel replaces IIS, but in production they often work together. Let me explain both.

Kestrel vs IIS — not a competition:

Kestrel is ASP.NET Core's built-in, cross-platform web server. It runs your .NET app directly, handles HTTP connections, and is the default server for all .NET environments (Windows, Linux, macOS, Docker). It's fast, lightweight, and maintained by the ASP.NET Core team.

IIS (Internet Information Services) is Windows-only and has been the traditional Microsoft web server for decades. It handles SSL termination, Windows Auth, request queueing, process management, and serves as a robust reverse proxy.

Reverse Proxy concept:

In production, you typically put a battle-hardened server like IIS, Nginx, or Azure Application Gateway in front of Kestrel. This is the reverse proxy pattern:

Internet → [IIS / Nginx / Azure App Gateway] → [Kestrel (your .NET app)]
            (reverse proxy)                       (app server)

Reverse proxy handles:
• SSL termination (HTTPS → HTTP to Kestrel)
• Port 80/443 binding
• Static file serving
• Load balancing across multiple Kestrel instances
• DDoS protection, rate limiting at network level
• Compression, caching headers
• Multiple apps on same server (different paths/domains)
• Process management (restart Kestrel if it crashes)

Why not expose Kestrel directly to the internet?

  • Kestrel is optimised for ASP.NET Core — it's not a general-purpose hardened web server.
  • It lacks Windows Auth, kernel-mode request handling, and decades of security hardening that IIS has.
  • Reverse proxy adds a security layer — the internet never directly reaches your app server.
  • With Nginx on Linux: one Nginx instance can reverse-proxy 10 different .NET microservices on different ports.

In-process vs out-of-process hosting with IIS:

<!-- web.config -->
<aspNetCore processPath="dotnet" arguments="MyApp.dll"
    hostingModel="inprocess" />
<!--
  inprocess (default): IIS worker process hosts the .NET app directly.
  Fastest option, one process.

  outofprocess: IIS reverse-proxies to a separate Kestrel process.
  More isolated, better for legacy/migration scenarios.
-->

On Linux (typical production):

# Nginx config -- reverse proxy to Kestrel on port 5000
server {
    listen 80;
    server_name myapp.com;

    location / {
        proxy_pass         http://localhost:5000;
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header   Upgrade $http_upgrade;
        proxy_set_header   Connection keep-alive;
        proxy_set_header   Host $host;
        proxy_set_header   X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
    }
}

followUps.question: What is UseForwardedHeaders() and why do you need it with a reverse proxy?
followUps.answer:
When Kestrel sits behind a reverse proxy, the original client IP and HTTPS scheme are lost — Kestrel only sees the proxy's local IP and HTTP (because SSL is terminated at the proxy). This breaks things like Request.IsHttps, redirect URLs, and IP-based logging.

UseForwardedHeaders() reads the X-Forwarded-For and X-Forwarded-Proto headers that the proxy sets, and uses them to populate HttpContext.Connection.RemoteIpAddress and Request.Scheme correctly.

app.UseForwardedHeaders(new ForwardedHeadersOptions
{
    ForwardedHeaders = ForwardedHeaders.XForwardedFor | ForwardedHeaders.XForwardedProto
});
// Must be FIRST middleware — before any code that reads Request.Scheme or IP

Without this, your HTTPS redirects might loop, generated URLs might use HTTP, and audit logs will show the proxy IP instead of the real client IP.

questions.tip: Docker containers usually skip the reverse proxy — instead, Azure App Gateway, an Ingress controller (in Kubernetes), or Azure Front Door sits in front of the container. The container runs Kestrel directly. Same architecture, different components.

questions.question: What is the difference between Web API and WCF? When would you still use WCF?
questions.answer:
WCF (Windows Communication Foundation) was Microsoft's pre-Web-API framework for building services. In any interview at a company with legacy .NET Framework code, this question will come up. My honest answer: WCF is a legacy technology. New projects should use Web API or gRPC. But I need to understand WCF to maintain existing systems.

Web API vs WCF — side by side:

AspectWeb APIWCF
ProtocolHTTP only (REST)HTTP, TCP, Named Pipes, MSMQ
Message formatJSON, XMLXML/SOAP (primary), can do REST
PlatformCross-platform (.NET, Node, mobile)Windows-heavy, some Linux via CoreWCF
ContractURL + HTTP verbExplicit contracts via [ServiceContract], [OperationContract]
ConfigCode-first, minimalComplex XML config (app.config)
Modern?Current standard ✅Legacy, Microsoft deprecated ❌

WCF architecture (for legacy understanding):

// WCF Service Contract
[ServiceContract]
public interface IOrderService
{
    [OperationContract]
    Order GetOrder(int id);

    [OperationContract]
    void CreateOrder(Order order);
}

// WCF Service Implementation
public class OrderService : IOrderService
{
    public Order GetOrder(int id) { ... }
    public void CreateOrder(Order order) { ... }
}

// WCF uses SOAP envelope -- XML heavy
// POST /OrderService.svc
// Content-Type: text/xml
// <s:Envelope><s:Body><GetOrder><id>1</id></GetOrder></s:Body></s:Envelope>

// Web API equivalent -- clean and simple
// GET /api/orders/1
// Returns: { "id": 1, "status": "Pending" }

When you'd still encounter WCF:

  • Maintaining legacy .NET Framework enterprise applications (banking, insurance).
  • When the client system expects SOAP/WSDL contracts and can't be changed.
  • TCP binding for high-performance internal services (though gRPC has superseded this).
  • Named Pipes for inter-process communication on the same machine.
  • Message queue integration with MSMQ (though modern apps use Azure Service Bus/RabbitMQ instead).

Migration path:

If you're maintaining a WCF service and need to modernise: CoreWCF is an open-source port of WCF to .NET Core/5+ for running legacy WCF services without rewriting them. For new features, write them as Web API or gRPC endpoints alongside the existing WCF service.

followUps.question: What is SOAP and how is it different from REST?
followUps.answer:
SOAP (Simple Object Access Protocol) is a protocol with strict XML-based message format, mandatory envelope structure, and WSDL contract definition. It supports built-in error handling (SOAP faults), transactions, and security (WS-Security). Heavy, verbose, but formally specified.

REST is an architectural style (not a protocol) that uses HTTP verbs and URLs to represent resources. Messages are typically JSON (lightweight). No formal contract required (though OpenAPI/Swagger documents it). Stateless by design.

SOAP example message: 150+ bytes of XML for 'give me user with id 1'. REST: GET /api/users/1. The difference in payload size and simplicity is why REST won the web. SOAP remains in B2B integrations (banking, government) where formal contracts and guaranteed delivery matter.

questions.tip: You'll encounter WCF in interviews at companies with 10+ year old .NET systems. Know the conceptual difference (SOAP vs REST, contract-first vs URL-based) but don't deep-dive into WCF config — interviewers mostly want to know you understand WHY Web API replaced it.

questions.question: What is HTTP stateless protocol? Why does that mean we need session management?
questions.answer:
Understanding why HTTP is stateless is the foundation of understanding why cookies, sessions, and tokens exist at all. This question sounds basic but the interviewer wants to see that you understand the WHY behind session management, not just the HOW.

What stateless means:

Every HTTP request is completely independent. The server treats each request as if it came from a stranger — it has no memory of previous requests. When you make Request 1 (login) and then Request 2 (view your profile), the server doesn't automatically know that Request 2 is from the same user who just logged in.

Request 1: POST /login  { username: "darshan", password: "xxxx" }
Server processes it, authenticates the user... but then forgets.

Request 2: GET /dashboard
Server has NO IDEA this is the same Darshan who just logged in.
Without state management, every request needs re-authentication.

Why this is by design (not a flaw):

  • Scalability — any server in a load-balanced cluster can handle any request. No need to route Darshan's requests to the same server every time.
  • Simplicity — the server doesn't need to track millions of ongoing conversations. Each request is self-contained.
  • Reliability — if one server dies, the next server can serve the next request with no disruption.

Solutions for managing state over stateless HTTP:

// 1. SESSIONS -- server-side state identified by a cookie
// Server stores user data, client holds a session ID cookie
// Session cookie: { .ASPXSESSION: "abc123" }
// Server looks up "abc123" in session store, finds Darshan's data
HttpContext.Session.SetString("UserId", "42");
var userId = HttpContext.Session.GetString("UserId");

// 2. COOKIES -- client-side state
// Server sends a Set-Cookie header, browser sends it back on every request
Response.Cookies.Append("Preference", "dark-mode",
    new CookieOptions { Expires = DateTimeOffset.UtcNow.AddDays(30) });
var pref = Request.Cookies["Preference"];

// 3. JWT TOKENS -- stateless authentication (my preferred approach for APIs)
// Token contains the user's identity, signed by the server
// Client sends it in Authorization header on every request
// No server-side storage needed -- token is self-describing
// Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCJ9...

// 4. HIDDEN FORM FIELDS -- state passed in forms
<input type="hidden" name="orderId" value="99" />

// 5. URL QUERY STRINGS -- state in the URL (for non-sensitive, shareable state)
// GET /orders?status=pending&page=2

The key trade-off: server-side vs client-side state:

  • Server-side (Session) — more secure (data on server, not exposed), harder to scale (need shared session store like Redis), works with any client.
  • Client-side (JWT/Cookie) — scales perfectly (server is stateless, any instance handles any request), but data is visible to the client (JWT is Base64-encoded, not encrypted), and can't be invalidated without extra infrastructure.

followUps.question: What is the difference between HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3?
followUps.answer:
HTTP/1.1 — text-based, one request per TCP connection at a time (sequential). Head-of-line blocking: a slow response blocks all others on the same connection. Fixed with multiple connections per domain (browsers open 6-8 in parallel).

HTTP/2 — binary protocol, multiplexing (multiple requests/responses over one connection simultaneously), header compression (HPACK), server push. Significantly faster for page loads. Required for gRPC. Most web traffic is HTTP/2 today.

HTTP/3 — runs over QUIC (UDP-based) instead of TCP. Eliminates TCP head-of-line blocking at the transport layer. Even better for unreliable networks (mobile, lossy connections). Still rolling out — supported by major browsers and CDNs.

In ASP.NET Core: HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 are supported by Kestrel natively. Enable HTTP/3 with builder.WebHost.ConfigureKestrel(opts => opts.ListenAnyIP(5001, lo => lo.UseHttps().Protocols = HttpProtocols.Http1AndHttp2AndHttp3)).

questions.tip: For APIs — use JWT, not sessions. Sessions require server-side storage and don't scale horizontally without Redis. JWT is self-contained and stateless, matching HTTP's stateless nature. Use sessions for web apps where cookie-based auth makes sense.

questions.question: What is the difference between .NET SDK and .NET Runtime?
questions.answer:
This trips up a lot of people. Here's how I think about it:

.NET Runtime

The minimum required to run a .NET application. It includes:

  • The CLR (Common Language Runtime) — manages memory, garbage collection, JIT compilation
  • The base class libraries (BCL) needed at runtime

When you deploy a .NET app to a production server, you only need the runtime — not the SDK.

.NET SDK (Software Development Kit)

Everything in the Runtime plus everything you need to build and develop:

  • The dotnet CLI (dotnet build, dotnet run, dotnet publish)
  • The Roslyn compiler (C# compiler)
  • NuGet package manager
  • Project templates
  • MSBuild

Practical implications:

// Developer machine: install SDK
dotnet new webapi -n MyApi      // needs SDK
dotnet build                    // needs SDK
dotnet publish -c Release       // needs SDK

// Production server: only runtime needed
// The published output is self-contained or framework-dependent

Self-contained vs framework-dependent deployments:

# Framework-dependent: smaller output, needs runtime installed on server
dotnet publish -c Release --runtime win-x64

# Self-contained: larger but no runtime needed on server
dotnet publish -c Release --runtime win-x64 --self-contained true

In CI/CD pipelines I use the SDK image for build stages and the runtime image for deployment stages — e.g., mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/sdk:8.0 for build and mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/aspnet:8.0 for the final Docker image. This keeps production images lean.

followUps.question: What is self-contained deployment vs framework-dependent?
What is the difference between .NET 8 LTS and STS versions?
What is AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compilation in .NET?

questions.tip: SDK = build tools + runtime. Runtime only = run, not build. Production servers only need the runtime.

questions.question: What is the difference between a DLL and an EXE in .NET? What are the types of assemblies?
questions.answer:
Both DLL and EXE are assemblies in .NET — PE (Portable Executable) files containing IL (Intermediate Language) code. The difference is in how they're used:

EXE (Executable Assembly)

  • Has an entry point (Main method / top-level statements)
  • Can be run directly by the OS
  • In .NET, a console app or a host process is an EXE

DLL (Dynamic Link Library)

  • No entry point — cannot be run directly
  • Loaded by an EXE (or another DLL) at runtime
  • Class libraries, NuGet packages, ASP.NET Core apps published as DLLs

Types of .NET Assemblies

1. Private Assembly
Installed in the application's own folder. No registration needed. Most assemblies in modern .NET are private.

// Your project references — output to bin/ folder
MyApp.dll         // your app
MyApp.Services.dll  // your class library

2. Shared Assembly (Strong-Named)
Installed in the GAC (Global Assembly Cache) so multiple apps can share it. Requires a strong name (public/private key pair). Common in .NET Framework — less relevant in .NET Core/5+ which moved away from the GAC.

// Strong-naming with sn.exe
[assembly: AssemblyKeyFile("mykey.snk")]

3. Satellite Assembly
Contains only resources (strings, images) for localization. Named with culture code: MyApp.resources.dll in en-US/ folders.

In modern .NET (Core/5+), the GAC is gone. All assemblies are effectively private, versioned via NuGet, and isolated per application. Strong naming is still supported but mainly for backward compatibility and security signing scenarios.

followUps.question: What is the GAC and is it still used in .NET Core?
What is a strong-named assembly?
What is the difference between static and dynamic linking?

questions.tip: GAC is .NET Framework legacy. In .NET 5+ everything is private/NuGet-based — mention this distinction.

questions.question: What is DelegatingHandler in ASP.NET Web API? How does it differ from ASP.NET Core Middleware?
questions.answer:
DelegatingHandler is a message handler in the classic ASP.NET Web API 2 (not Core) pipeline. It sits in the HTTP message pipeline and can inspect/modify requests and responses — similar in concept to middleware, but different API.

DelegatingHandler in Web API 2 (legacy):

public class LoggingHandler : DelegatingHandler
{
    protected override async Task<HttpResponseMessage> SendAsync(
        HttpRequestMessage request, 
        CancellationToken cancellationToken)
    {
        // Before the request
        Console.WriteLine($"Request: {request.Method} {request.RequestUri}");
        
        // Call the next handler in the chain
        HttpResponseMessage response = await base.SendAsync(request, cancellationToken);
        
        // After the response
        Console.WriteLine($"Response: {response.StatusCode}");
        
        return response;
    }
}

// Registration in WebApiConfig.cs
config.MessageHandlers.Add(new LoggingHandler());

Equivalent in ASP.NET Core — Middleware (modern approach):

public class LoggingMiddleware
{
    private readonly RequestDelegate _next;
    private readonly ILogger<LoggingMiddleware> _logger;

    public LoggingMiddleware(RequestDelegate next, ILogger<LoggingMiddleware> logger)
    {
        _next = next;
        _logger = logger;
    }

    public async Task InvokeAsync(HttpContext context)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Request: {Method} {Path}", 
            context.Request.Method, context.Request.Path);
        
        await _next(context);  // call next middleware
        
        _logger.LogInformation("Response: {StatusCode}", 
            context.Response.StatusCode);
    }
}

// Registration in Program.cs
app.UseMiddleware<LoggingMiddleware>();

Key differences:

DelegatingHandler (Web API 2)Middleware (ASP.NET Core)
Framework.NET Framework / Web API 2ASP.NET Core
Works withHttpRequestMessage / HttpResponseMessageHttpContext
Registrationconfig.MessageHandlers.Add()app.UseMiddleware<>()
DI supportLimitedFull DI via constructor
StatusLegacyCurrent standard

In any new .NET project (Core/5/6/7/8), you use middleware. DelegatingHandler is only relevant if you're maintaining a legacy .NET Framework Web API 2 application. If someone asks this in a .NET Core context, I'd mention that middleware is the ASP.NET Core equivalent and explain how it differs.

followUps.question: What is the difference between middleware and action filters in ASP.NET Core?
How do you create a typed HttpClient with DelegatingHandler in ASP.NET Core?
What are the use cases for HTTP message handlers?

questions.tip: In ASP.NET Core, DelegatingHandler still exists but for HttpClient pipelines (typed clients), not the server pipeline. Server-side uses middleware.

questions.question: What is HATEOAS and how does it relate to REST API design?
questions.answer:
HATEOAS stands for Hypermedia As The Engine Of Application State. It's one of Roy Fielding's original REST constraints — the idea that API responses should include links to related actions, so clients can navigate the API dynamically without hard-coding URLs.

What it looks like in practice:

// Without HATEOAS — client must know all URLs upfront
GET /api/orders/123
{
  "orderId": 123,
  "status": "pending",
  "total": 250.00
}

// With HATEOAS — response tells client what it can do next
GET /api/orders/123
{
  "orderId": 123,
  "status": "pending",
  "total": 250.00,
  "_links": {
    "self":    { "href": "/api/orders/123",        "method": "GET"    },
    "cancel":  { "href": "/api/orders/123/cancel", "method": "DELETE" },
    "pay":     { "href": "/api/orders/123/pay",    "method": "POST"   },
    "customer":{ "href": "/api/customers/456",     "method": "GET"    }
  }
}

Implementing in ASP.NET Core:

public class OrderDto
{
    public int OrderId { get; set; }
    public string Status { get; set; }
    public decimal Total { get; set; }
    public List<Link> Links { get; set; } = new();
}

public class Link
{
    public string Href { get; set; }
    public string Rel { get; set; }
    public string Method { get; set; }
}

// In controller:
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public ActionResult<OrderDto> GetOrder(int id)
{
    var order = _orderService.GetById(id);
    var dto = _mapper.Map<OrderDto>(order);
    
    dto.Links.Add(new Link { Href = $"/api/orders/{id}", Rel = "self", Method = "GET" });
    
    if (order.Status == "pending")
    {
        dto.Links.Add(new Link { Href = $"/api/orders/{id}/cancel", Rel = "cancel", Method = "DELETE" });
        dto.Links.Add(new Link { Href = $"/api/orders/{id}/pay", Rel = "pay", Method = "POST" });
    }
    
    return Ok(dto);
}

Honest take:

HATEOAS is theoretically elegant but rarely fully implemented in practice. Most real-world REST APIs are "REST-like" and skip HATEOAS. The value is highest when building public APIs consumed by clients you don't control. For internal APIs within a company, the overhead usually isn't worth it. I've seen it used in payment APIs (where the available actions change based on order state) and banking APIs.

followUps.question: Is HATEOAS required for a truly RESTful API?
What is Richardson Maturity Model?
Which .NET libraries help implement HATEOAS?

questions.tip: Richardson Maturity Model: Level 0=RPC, Level 1=Resources, Level 2=HTTP verbs, Level 3=HATEOAS. Most real APIs are at Level 2.

questions.question: What is idempotency in REST? Which HTTP methods are idempotent and why does it matter?
questions.answer:
An operation is idempotent if making the same request multiple times produces the same result as making it once. This concept is crucial for building reliable APIs, especially when handling retries after network failures.

Which methods are idempotent?

MethodIdempotent?Safe?Why
GET✅ Yes✅ YesRead-only, same result every time
PUT✅ Yes❌ NoReplace resource — calling twice with same data = same state
DELETE✅ Yes❌ NoFirst call deletes, subsequent calls still return 404 — end state is "deleted"
PATCH⚠️ Depends❌ NoCan be idempotent if absolute values, not if relative (+10 to balance)
POST❌ No❌ NoCreates a new resource each time — calling POST twice creates two records
HEAD, OPTIONS✅ Yes✅ YesRead-only metadata

Why it matters — Retry logic:

// Client makes PUT /api/orders/123 — network drops
// Client retries the same PUT — SAFE because PUT is idempotent
// The order is updated once, regardless of how many retries

// Client makes POST /api/orders — network drops  
// Client retries — DANGEROUS — creates a duplicate order!
// Solution: use idempotency keys

// POST with idempotency key header:
POST /api/orders
Idempotency-Key: uuid-abc-123-def

// Server stores processed idempotency keys:
// If same key arrives again, return the cached response — don't re-process

Implementing idempotency keys for POST in ASP.NET Core:

[HttpPost]
public async Task<IActionResult> CreateOrder(
    [FromBody] CreateOrderRequest request,
    [FromHeader(Name = "Idempotency-Key")] string? idempotencyKey)
{
    if (idempotencyKey != null)
    {
        var cached = await _cache.GetAsync<OrderDto>($"idem:{idempotencyKey}");
        if (cached != null) return Ok(cached); // replay cached response
    }
    
    var order = await _orderService.CreateAsync(request);
    
    if (idempotencyKey != null)
        await _cache.SetAsync($"idem:{idempotencyKey}", order, TimeSpan.FromHours(24));
    
    return CreatedAtAction(nameof(GetOrder), new { id = order.Id }, order);
}

Payment APIs like Stripe use idempotency keys extensively. I've used the same pattern in financial transaction APIs where duplicate charges are catastrophic.

followUps.question: What is the difference between safe and idempotent?
How would you implement idempotency keys in a distributed system?
Is PATCH always idempotent?

questions.tip: POST is not idempotent. For safe POST retries, use idempotency keys — Stripe pioneered this pattern.

questions.question: What is the difference between REST and GraphQL? When would you choose one over the other?
questions.answer:
Both REST and GraphQL are ways to expose data over HTTP. The key difference is in how clients request data.

REST

  • Multiple endpoints, each returns a fixed shape of data
  • Client has no control over what fields are returned
  • Can lead to over-fetching (getting fields you don't need) or under-fetching (needing multiple calls)
// REST — client gets everything, even if it only needs name and email
GET /api/users/123
// Returns: id, name, email, address, phone, createdAt, roles, preferences...

// REST — need 3 calls to build a dashboard
GET /api/users/123
GET /api/orders?userId=123
GET /api/notifications?userId=123

GraphQL

  • Single endpoint (/graphql), client specifies exactly what fields it needs
  • Eliminates over-fetching and under-fetching
  • Strongly typed schema
// GraphQL — client asks for exactly what it needs
POST /graphql
{
  user(id: 123) {
    name
    email
    orders(last: 5) {
      id
      total
    }
    unreadNotifications {
      count
    }
  }
}
// One request, exactly the data needed

In ASP.NET Core — Hot Chocolate library:

// GraphQL in .NET with Hot Chocolate
builder.Services.AddGraphQLServer()
    .AddQueryType<Query>()
    .AddMutationType<Mutation>();

public class Query
{
    public User GetUser([Service] IUserService svc, int id) 
        => svc.GetById(id);
}

When to choose:

Use REST when...Use GraphQL when...
Simple CRUD operationsComplex data with many relationships
Public API (easier to document and consume)Multiple clients need different data shapes (mobile vs web)
Caching matters (HTTP caching works natively)Reducing network round-trips is critical
Team familiar with RESTAggregating data from multiple services

In most .NET projects I've worked on, REST is the default choice — simpler to implement, Swagger integration is mature, and most teams know it well. I'd reach for GraphQL if I had multiple client types (mobile, web, partner APIs) each needing different slices of the same data.

followUps.question: What is the N+1 problem in GraphQL and how do you solve it with DataLoader?
How does HTTP caching work with GraphQL?
What is gRPC and when would you use it over REST or GraphQL?

questions.tip: REST has better HTTP caching, simpler tooling. GraphQL solves over-fetching/under-fetching. Pick based on client diversity and data complexity.

questions.question: What is the difference between query parameters and path parameters in REST API? When do you use each?
questions.answer:
This comes up constantly in API design discussions. The rule is simple once you understand the intent.

Path Parameters — identify a specific resource

Embedded in the URL path itself. Used when the parameter identifies which resource you're accessing. They are mandatory.

// Path parameter: {id} identifies the specific order
GET /api/orders/456        → Get order with ID 456
DELETE /api/orders/456     → Delete order 456
PUT /api/orders/456        → Update order 456

// Nested resource
GET /api/customers/123/orders/456  → Order 456 belonging to customer 123

// In ASP.NET Core:
[HttpGet("{id}")]
public IActionResult GetOrder(int id) { ... }

[HttpGet("{customerId}/orders/{orderId}")]
public IActionResult GetCustomerOrder(int customerId, int orderId) { ... }

Query Parameters — filter, sort, paginate, search

Appended after ? in the URL. Used for optional modifiers on the resource collection. They are optional.

// Filtering
GET /api/orders?status=pending&customerId=123

// Pagination
GET /api/orders?page=2&pageSize=20

// Sorting
GET /api/products?sort=price&order=asc

// Searching
GET /api/customers?search=john&city=Mumbai

// Multiple filters combined
GET /api/orders?status=delivered&dateFrom=2024-01-01&dateTo=2024-12-31

// In ASP.NET Core:
[HttpGet]
public IActionResult GetOrders(
    [FromQuery] string? status,
    [FromQuery] int page = 1,
    [FromQuery] int pageSize = 20,
    [FromQuery] string? sort = "createdAt"
) { ... }

Common mistake to avoid:

// BAD — don't use path params for optional filters
GET /api/orders/pending/page/2/sort/date  ❌

// GOOD — path param for ID, query params for everything else
GET /api/orders/456?includeItems=true     ✅
GET /api/orders?status=pending&page=2     ✅

Decision rule:

  • Path param → "Which resource?" (required, identifies the entity)
  • Query param → "How do you want it?" (optional, filters/modifies the response)

followUps.question: When would you use a request body instead of query parameters?
Can you use query parameters with DELETE or PUT?
What is the maximum URL length and does it affect query parameter usage?

questions.tip: Path params = identity (required). Query params = filtering/sorting/pagination (optional). Never put sensitive data like passwords in query params — they appear in server logs.

questions.question: What are REST API best practices? What are the key design principles you follow?
questions.answer:
After building and consuming many APIs, here are the principles I apply consistently:

1. Use nouns for resources, not verbs

// BAD — verbs in URL
POST /api/createOrder
GET  /api/getOrders
POST /api/deleteOrder/123

// GOOD — nouns + HTTP method conveys the action
POST   /api/orders          // create
GET    /api/orders          // list
GET    /api/orders/123      // get one
PUT    /api/orders/123      // update
DELETE /api/orders/123      // delete

2. Use proper HTTP status codes

200 OK          → successful GET, PUT, PATCH
201 Created     → successful POST (include Location header)
204 No Content  → successful DELETE (no response body)
400 Bad Request → validation failures
401 Unauthorized→ not authenticated
403 Forbidden   → authenticated but no permission
404 Not Found   → resource doesn't exist
409 Conflict    → duplicate or state conflict
422 Unprocessable → business rule violation
429 Too Many    → rate limit exceeded
500 Server Error→ something we didn't handle

3. Version your API from day one

// URL versioning (most visible, easiest to route)
GET /api/v1/orders
GET /api/v2/orders

// Header versioning
GET /api/orders
Api-Version: 2.0

4. Return consistent error responses

// Consistent error envelope (use ProblemDetails in ASP.NET Core)
{
  "type": "https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc7231#section-6.5.1",
  "title": "Validation failed",
  "status": 400,
  "errors": {
    "Email": ["Email is required"],
    "Age": ["Age must be 18 or older"]
  }
}

5. Pagination on collections

GET /api/orders?page=1&pageSize=20

// Response includes pagination metadata
{
  "data": [...],
  "pagination": {
    "page": 1,
    "pageSize": 20,
    "totalCount": 847,
    "totalPages": 43
  }
}

6. Other rules I follow

  • Use plural nouns: /orders not /order
  • Use kebab-case for URLs: /order-items not /orderItems
  • Always use HTTPS in production
  • Support filtering, sorting, searching via query params
  • Use ETag + If-None-Match for caching GET responses
  • Document with Swagger/OpenAPI — in ASP.NET Core this is just a NuGet package away
  • Implement rate limiting on public endpoints
  • Log request/response with correlation IDs for tracing

followUps.question: What is the ProblemDetails standard in ASP.NET Core?
What is HATEOAS and do you use it?
How do you handle breaking vs non-breaking API changes?

questions.tip: The most common mistake I see: using POST for everything. Proper HTTP verbs + meaningful status codes = self-documenting API.

questions.question: What is Swagger/OpenAPI? How do you implement it in ASP.NET Core?
questions.answer:
Swagger (now standardized as OpenAPI Specification) is a tool that automatically generates interactive API documentation from your code. It gives developers a UI where they can see all endpoints, request/response schemas, and even test the API directly in the browser — essential for any team.

Implementation in ASP.NET Core using Swashbuckle:

// 1. Install NuGet package
// Swashbuckle.AspNetCore

// 2. Register in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddEndpointsApiExplorer(); // required for minimal APIs
builder.Services.AddSwaggerGen(options =>
{
    options.SwaggerDoc("v1", new OpenApiInfo
    {
        Title = "My API",
        Version = "v1",
        Description = "Interview Prep Application API",
        Contact = new OpenApiContact { Name = "Darshan", Email = "dev@company.com" }
    });

    // Include XML comments from controller actions
    var xmlFile = $"{Assembly.GetExecutingAssembly().GetName().Name}.xml";
    var xmlPath = Path.Combine(AppContext.BaseDirectory, xmlFile);
    options.IncludeXmlComments(xmlPath);

    // Add JWT auth support in Swagger UI
    options.AddSecurityDefinition("Bearer", new OpenApiSecurityScheme
    {
        Type = SecuritySchemeType.Http,
        Scheme = "bearer",
        BearerFormat = "JWT",
        Description = "Enter JWT token"
    });
    options.AddSecurityRequirement(new OpenApiSecurityRequirement
    {
        [
            new OpenApiSecurityScheme
            {
                Reference = new OpenApiReference { Type = ReferenceType.SecurityScheme, Id = "Bearer" }
            }
        ] = Array.Empty<string>()
    });
});

// 3. Add middleware — usually only in development
if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment())
{
    app.UseSwagger();       // generates /swagger/v1/swagger.json
    app.UseSwaggerUI(c =>  // serves the UI at /swagger
    {
        c.SwaggerEndpoint("/swagger/v1/swagger.json", "My API V1");
        c.RoutePrefix = string.Empty; // serve at root URL
    });
}

// 4. Decorate controllers with XML docs for rich descriptions
/// <summary>Get a product by ID</summary>
/// <param name="id">Product identifier</param>
/// <response code="200">Returns the product</response>
/// <response code="404">Product not found</response>
[HttpGet("{id}")]
[ProducesResponseType(typeof(ProductDto), 200)]
[ProducesResponseType(404)]
public async Task<IActionResult> GetProduct(int id) { ... }

Security tip for production: Don't expose Swagger publicly. Either disable it in production or protect the /swagger route with auth middleware:

// Only enable in Development OR with an explicit feature flag
if (app.Environment.IsDevelopment() || config.GetValue<bool>("EnableSwagger"))
{
    app.UseSwagger();
    app.UseSwaggerUI();
}

followUps.question: How do you version your API and expose multiple Swagger documents for each version?
What is the difference between Swashbuckle and NSwag?
How do you secure Swagger UI in non-development environments?

questions.tip: Swashbuckle auto-generates from controller attributes and XML docs. Always add [ProducesResponseType] attributes for accurate schemas. In production, either disable Swagger or protect it with auth — never leave it open.

questions.question: What is IHostedService in ASP.NET Core? How do you use BackgroundService?
questions.answer:
IHostedService is the interface for running background work tied to the ASP.NET Core application lifecycle. It starts when the app starts and stops (cleanly) when the app shuts down. Think of it as the .NET Core equivalent of Windows Services.

IHostedService has two methods: StartAsync(CancellationToken) and StopAsync(CancellationToken). For long-running loops, you'd typically fire off a background task in StartAsync.

BackgroundService is the abstract base class that handles the boilerplate — you just override ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken) and write your loop:

// Long-running background worker — queue processor
public class OrderProcessingService : BackgroundService
{
    private readonly ILogger<OrderProcessingService> _logger;
    private readonly IServiceScopeFactory _scopeFactory; // for scoped services

    public OrderProcessingService(ILogger<OrderProcessingService> logger,
        IServiceScopeFactory scopeFactory)
    {
        _logger = logger;
        _scopeFactory = scopeFactory;
    }

    protected override async Task ExecuteAsync(CancellationToken stoppingToken)
    {
        _logger.LogInformation("Order processor started");

        while (!stoppingToken.IsCancellationRequested)
        {
            try
            {
                await ProcessPendingOrdersAsync(stoppingToken);
            }
            catch (Exception ex) when (ex is not OperationCanceledException)
            {
                _logger.LogError(ex, "Error processing orders");
            }

            await Task.Delay(TimeSpan.FromSeconds(30), stoppingToken); // wait 30s
        }

        _logger.LogInformation("Order processor stopped");
    }

    private async Task ProcessPendingOrdersAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // IMPORTANT: BackgroundService is Singleton — can't inject Scoped services directly
        // Use IServiceScopeFactory to create a scope per execution
        using var scope = _scopeFactory.CreateScope();
        var dbContext = scope.ServiceProvider.GetRequiredService<AppDbContext>();

        var pending = await dbContext.Orders
            .Where(o => o.Status == OrderStatus.Pending)
            .ToListAsync(ct);

        foreach (var order in pending)
        {
            order.Status = OrderStatus.Processing;
            _logger.LogInformation("Processing order {Id}", order.Id);
        }
        await dbContext.SaveChangesAsync(ct);
    }
}

// Register in Program.cs
builder.Services.AddHostedService<OrderProcessingService>();

// For fire-and-forget one-time startup tasks, use IHostedService directly
public class DatabaseSeedService : IHostedService
{
    public async Task StartAsync(CancellationToken ct)
    {
        // Run once at startup — seed data, warm cache, etc.
        await SeedDatabaseAsync(ct);
    }
    public Task StopAsync(CancellationToken ct) => Task.CompletedTask;
}

Key gotcha: BackgroundService is registered as Singleton. You can't inject Scoped services (like DbContext) directly into its constructor. Always use IServiceScopeFactory to create a scope per execution.

followUps.question: Why can't you inject a Scoped service directly into BackgroundService?
How do you gracefully stop a BackgroundService during app shutdown?
What is the difference between IHostedService and a Windows Service?

questions.tip: BackgroundService = abstract base class for long-running loops. Register with AddHostedService<T>(). Critical: it's Singleton — use IServiceScopeFactory to resolve Scoped services like DbContext per execution cycle.

questions.question: What are HTTP status codes? Explain the main categories and common codes.
questions.answer:
HTTP status codes tell the client what happened with their request. Every API developer needs to know these — using the wrong code (e.g., returning 200 for an error) makes your API confusing to consume.

5 categories:

RangeCategoryMeaning
1xxInformationalRequest received, processing continues
2xxSuccessRequest completed successfully
3xxRedirectionFurther action needed
4xxClient ErrorProblem with the request
5xxServer ErrorServer failed to process valid request

Most important codes in practice:

// ASP.NET Core — returning appropriate status codes

// 200 OK — request succeeded with data
return Ok(product); // GET returns 200

// 201 Created — resource created, include Location header
return CreatedAtAction(nameof(GetProduct), new { id = product.Id }, product);

// 204 No Content — success but nothing to return (PUT, DELETE)
return NoContent();

// 400 Bad Request — invalid input/validation failed
if (!ModelState.IsValid) return BadRequest(ModelState);

// 401 Unauthorized — not authenticated (missing/invalid token)
// Returned automatically by [Authorize] when no token present

// 403 Forbidden — authenticated but not authorized
// Returned by [Authorize(Roles = "Admin")] when user lacks the role

// 404 Not Found — resource doesn't exist
if (product == null) return NotFound(new { message = "Product not found" });

// 405 Method Not Allowed — wrong HTTP verb
// ASP.NET Core handles this automatically based on [HttpGet]/[HttpPost]

// 409 Conflict — business rule violation (duplicate, optimistic concurrency)
return Conflict(new { message = "Username already taken" });

// 422 Unprocessable Entity — syntactically valid but semantically wrong
return UnprocessableEntity(new { message = "Age cannot be negative" });

// 429 Too Many Requests — rate limit exceeded
// Return with Retry-After header

// 500 Internal Server Error — unexpected server failure
// Never return this intentionally — let global exception handler emit it

// 503 Service Unavailable — server down/overloaded
// Used in health check endpoints

Common mistake: Don't return 200 with an { "success": false } body for errors. Use the right 4xx code — it makes your API predictable and tools like Swagger and API clients handle it correctly.

followUps.question: What is the difference between 401 Unauthorized and 403 Forbidden?
When should you use 422 Unprocessable Entity vs 400 Bad Request?
What HTTP status code should a DELETE return if the resource doesn't exist?

questions.tip: 401 = not authenticated (who are you?). 403 = authenticated but not allowed (I know who you are, but no). 404 = resource not found. 409 = conflict/business rule. Never wrap errors in 200 OK responses.
